# VantaUI CSS

> A dark, angular, military-telemetry **tactical HUD** CSS library. Semantic-first, similar to BeerCSS: you write standard, plain semantic HTML to get a tactical UI without using component classes. Reach for one short helper word only to deviate. Responsive by default, OKLCH colors, zero runtime dependencies, framework-agnostic (Vue / Nuxt / vanilla). Optional tiny JS adds tabs, animated meters, drawers, dropdowns, toasts, tooltips, a live clock.

This file is a HUB. It carries the core information (rules, installation, and mental model) and links to focused topic files so you only read what is relevant to your current task. For instance, a nav bar needs the navigation file, not the whole library. Fetch the topic files by URL as needed; or read the single-file complete reference: https://vantaui-css.khodok.com/llms-full.txt. Authoritative as of v1.7.2.

## Critical facts (read first)

- **The package name is `vantaui-css`.** It is not published to npm or any CDN yet, so you should install it directly from GitHub (`github:Khoding/vantaui-css#<tag>`). All import specifiers start with `vantaui-css`.
- **You opt in with one class: `class="vui"`** on a root element (usually `<body>`). Inside `.vui`, plain semantic HTML is styled automatically. Outside `.vui`, only design tokens are defined, so nothing changes globally.
- **Semantic-first, almost zero classes.** A `<header>` containing a `<nav>` is an app bar. An `<article>` is a panel. A `<dialog class="left">` is a drawer. A `<meter>` is a telemetry bar. You rarely add classes; when you do, it is one short helper word (`glow`, `danger`, `small`, `left`).
- **All VantaUI styles live in cascade layers and are authored at zero specificity (`:where()`).** Your app's own (unlayered) classes ALWAYS win. You never need `!important` to override VantaUI.
- **`<i>text</i>` is an ICON** (Material Symbols ligature), e.g. `<i>home</i>` draws the home glyph. Use `<em>` for italic text, never `<i>`.
- Two prefixes exist: bare semantic styling needs **no prefix**; free-floating layout & utility helpers use the **`vui-`** prefix (collision-safe), e.g. `vui-grid`, `vui-p-4`, `vui-text-cyan`.
- CSS is fully usable with **no JavaScript**. JS only adds interactive niceties.

## Install

Since it is not on npm or a CDN yet, you can install it directly from GitHub by tag. This adds a dependency that resolves like `"vantaui-css": "github:Khoding/vantaui-css#v1.7.2"`:

```bash
npm i github:Khoding/vantaui-css#v1.7.2
```

```js
import 'vantaui-css';                     // the stylesheet (package "main"/"style")
import { init } from 'vantaui-css/js';    // optional behaviours
init();                                   // boots tabs, animated meters, drawers, menus, toolbars, tooltips, clock
```

```html
<!-- Optional faster icon path (also bundled via @import in the CSS): -->
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Material+Symbols+Outlined:opsz,wght,FILL,GRAD@24,400,0,0&display=block" />
<body class="vui">…</body>
```

Fonts (Chakra Petch · Rajdhani · Share Tech Mono · Material Symbols) load automatically via `@import` inside the stylesheet. Note that the remote `@import` can break some build tools (like LightningCSS). If this occurs, you can vendor a copy of the stylesheet with the `@import` line removed and load the fonts separately.

Package entry points (`exports`): `vantaui-css` (min css), `vantaui-css/css` (unminified), `vantaui-css/min`, `vantaui-css/js`, `vantaui-css/js/global`, `vantaui-css/vue`, `vantaui-css/nuxt`, `vantaui-css/src/*`.

## Mental model: Settings, Elements, and Helpers (the BeerCSS way)

- **Settings** = design tokens (CSS custom properties). Override any, anywhere because they cascade.
- **Elements** = semantic HTML styled directly inside `.vui`. No component classes.
- **Helpers** = one short element-scoped word to change a default (e.g. `<button class="danger">`).

State is semantic first: current nav item = `aria-current="page"` (or alias `class="active"`); invalid field = `aria-invalid`; a switch = `role="switch"`; alerts = `role="status"` / `role="alert"`; tabs = `role="tablist"`/`role="tab"`/`role="tabpanel"`.

### Minimal example

```html
<body class="vui">
  <header>
    <a href="/">VANTA<b>UI</b></a>
    <nav><a aria-current="page">Overview</a><a>Units</a></nav>
    <menu><button class="icon"><i>settings</i></button></menu>
  </header>
  <main>
    <h1>Command Center</h1>
    <article>
      <header><small class="vui-eyebrow">Diagnostics</small></header>
      <p>A chamfered panel. The header drew its own divider.</p>
      <footer><button type="submit">Authorize</button></footer>
    </article>
    <button>Override</button>
    <button class="danger">Abort</button>
    <label><input type="checkbox" role="switch" checked /> Active camo</label>
    <meter min="0" max="100" low="30" high="70" optimum="100" value="86"></meter>
  </main>
</body>
```

## App frame (quick reference)

Any `.vui` element that directly holds a `<main>` becomes an app frame and places landmarks by element, with no wrapper classes required:

```html
<body class="vui">
  <nav class="left">…</nav>   <!-- side rail: vertical ≥768px, bottom bar below -->
  <header>…</header>          <!-- top bar -->
  <main>…</main>              <!-- scrolling stage -->
  <footer>…</footer>          <!-- bottom bar (or use <nav class="bottom">) -->
</body>
```

On `<body>` it fills the viewport; nested in a sized box it fills the box. Full details (right rails, drawers, `header.inset`, `.vignette`) → the navigation file below.

## Reference (read only what your task needs)

- [Layout & utilities](https://vantaui-css.khodok.com/llms-layout.txt): breakpoints, container, flex composites, auto + 12-col grid, show/hide, the full utility-class catalogue, HUD primitives (type roles, plates, brackets, backdrops, status dot), prose layout.
- [Navigation & chrome](https://vantaui-css.khodok.com/llms-navigation.txt): app frame, header/app bar, footer, side rails, drawers (off-canvas + persistent), breadcrumbs, dropdown menus, tabs, pagination, toolbars.
- [Forms & buttons](https://vantaui-css.khodok.com/llms-forms.txt): buttons (tones/sizes/icon/groups), text fields, textarea, select, checkbox/radio, switch, range, field wrappers, validation.
- [Data display](https://vantaui-css.khodok.com/llms-data.txt): panels, tables, description lists, badges, avatars, key/value & stat readouts, timelines, steppers, trees, carousels, ratings, segmented controls.
- [Feedback & status](https://vantaui-css.khodok.com/llms-feedback.txt): inline alerts, toasts, meters & progress, radial gauges, spinners, skeletons, empty states, status dot.
- [Theming & tokens](https://vantaui-css.khodok.com/llms-theming.txt): the OKLCH color system and every design token, plus how to override them.
- [JavaScript & frameworks](https://vantaui-css.khodok.com/llms-js.txt): the optional behaviours module (tabs, meters, drawers, menus, toolbars, tooltips, clock, toast) and the Vue plugin + Nuxt module.
- [Everything in one file](https://vantaui-css.khodok.com/llms-full.txt): the complete reference, no link-following needed.

## Common mistakes to avoid

- Do NOT use `<i>` for italics; it renders an icon glyph. Use `<em>` instead.
- Do NOT add component classes like `.panel`, `.card`, or `.btn`. Use the semantic element (`<article>`, `<button>`). Helpers are single words, not BEM.
- Do NOT prefix bare semantic usage; the `vui-` prefix is only for free-floating layout/utility/primitive helpers.
- Do NOT use `!important` to override VantaUI. Write an unlayered class, which will always take precedence.
- The package is named `vantaui-css` and must be installed from GitHub (`github:Khoding/vantaui-css#<tag>`) because it is not yet available on npm or any CDN.
- The default `main` is the minified CSS; use `vantaui-css/css` for the unminified build.

## Browser support

Evergreen browsers from ~2023. Requires `:has()`, cascade layers, container queries, `color-mix()`, `oklch()`, `mask`, `conic-gradient`: Chrome/Edge 111+, Firefox 121+, Safari 16.4+. Some progressive enhancements (animated `<details>`, native CSS carousel controls, anchor-positioned popovers) light up on newer engines and degrade gracefully.
